subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s degree, likely in clinical research, public health, or a related biomedical field
Completed foundational coursework in research methodologies, biostatistics, and healthcare ethics
Attended specialized workshops on Good Clinical Practice (GCP) and regulatory compliance
Participates in continuing education to maintain certifications and stay current with industry standards

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Clinical Research Coordinator
Coordinates all phases of clinical trials, from patient recruitment to data management
Serves as the primary liaison between investigators, sponsors, and study participants
Oversees compliance with study protocols and regulatory requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the healthcare and life sciences sector
Specifically involved in clinical research and medical trials
Operates at the intersection of clinical care, research, and regulatory oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed by a mid-sized academic medical center or research hospital
Works within a multidisciplinary clinical research team (10-30 people)
Collaborates with both in-house and external partners (e.g., CROs, sponsors)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Organization conducts sponsored and investigator-initiated clinical trials
Focuses on pharmaceutical, device, and therapeutic studies
Serves both academic and commercial research sponsors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has over 10 years of experience as a Clinical Research Coordinator
Began career in clinical research coordination in early 2010s or before
Has witnessed significant changes in research regulations and technology adoption over time

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior-level coordinator position
Often mentors junior coordinators and provides input on protocol feasibility
Recognized as a subject matter expert within the research team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Reviews AI-generated trial updates and patient eligibility alerts each morning
Screens, recruits, and consents patients using digital platforms and eConsent tools
Monitors patient safety, adverse events, and protocol adherence via AI-powered dashboards
Manages regulatory documentation and data entry with automated reporting tools
Analyzes AI-generated reports to plan upcoming study tasks and optimize workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 30% of time on patient screening and enrollment activities
Devotes 25% of time to regulatory compliance and documentation tasks
Allocates 20% to patient safety monitoring and follow-up
Uses the remaining time for data analysis, team meetings, and process optimization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Exercises significant autonomy in day-to-day trial operations and scheduling
Makes independent decisions on patient eligibility, protocol adherence, and safety reporting
Collaborates with principal investigators for protocol deviations or complex cases
Responsible for escalating critical safety concerns to medical and regulatory teams

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Patient interaction and obtaining informed consent, especially with digital tools, are routine and comfortable
Data entry and regulatory documentation are well-practiced due to years of experience
Coordinating team meetings and communicating study updates come naturally

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Managing protocol amendments and ensuring full compliance with changing regulations is complex
Handling unexpected adverse events and patient safety escalations requires careful judgment
Integrating new AI tools with legacy systems sometimes presents technical challenges

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Routine data entry, especially for patient visit logs and regulatory forms, is highly repetitive
Preliminary patient eligibility screening based on EHR data is data-heavy and well-suited for AI automation
Automated adverse event tracking and reporting reduces manual workload

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys direct patient engagement and problem-solving during trial conduct
Finds protocol optimization and process improvement with AI tools intellectually stimulating
Considers repetitive paperwork and redundant documentation as tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Data entry errors and double documentation between different systems waste time
Delays in EHR data extraction or integration with trial management software can slow workflows
Manual follow-up for patient adherence and visit scheduling is inefficient without automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Tasks that align with expertise, such as protocol management, are both enjoyable and productive
High skill level in regulatory tasks leads to fewer errors and higher efficiency
Monotonous or low-skill tasks tend to lower engagement and overall productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open to learning new technologies if they offer clear workflow improvements
Approaches new tools with curiosity but prefers well-documented training resources
Values practical, hands-on learning over theoretical instruction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self reasonably adaptable, especially with structured onboarding
Leverages prior experience to quickly understand new clinical trial platforms
May require additional time for complex system integrations or major workflow shifts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to invest time in training when the technology is directly relevant to daily responsibilities
Prefers modular, on-demand training over lengthy classroom sessions
Balances time spent on training with ongoing trial commitments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential for increased trial efficiency and data quality
High workload during study start-up phases can limit time available for training
Skeptical of adopting tools that lack demonstrated value or user support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Positive peer experiences with a tool increase willingness to adopt
Management support and clear expectations facilitate smoother adoption
Peer-led training sessions are particularly effective for skill uptake

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses clinical trial management systems (CTMS) such as Medidata or REDCap daily
Accesses electronic health records (EHR) systems like Epic for patient data
Utilizes AI-powered patient screening and eConsent platforms
Employs secure cloud storage (e.g., Box, OneDrive) for regulatory documents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on spreadsheets (Excel) for tracking visit schedules and data reconciliation
Uses email and secure messaging for team and sponsor communications
Maintains paper-based backup for signed consent forms when required

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Experienced with EDC (Electronic Data Capture) platforms such as OpenClinica
Familiar with regulatory submission portals (e.g., IRBNet, FDA’s eCTD)
Comfortable with digital signatures and remote monitoring tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces challenges integrating AI tools with legacy CTMS systems
Data transfer between EHR and study databases can require manual reconciliation
Limited interoperability between sponsor-specific software and institutional platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI/ML terminology such as predictive modeling and natural language processing
Recognizes the distinction between rule-based automation and machine learning-driven insights
Has attended introductory webinars on AI in clinical research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses AI-powered patient prescreening tools (e.g., Deep 6 AI, IBM Watson Health)
Interacts with LLM-based digital assistants for regulatory query drafting
Employs AI modules integrated into CTMS for automated adverse event detection

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools occasionally, primarily for patient eligibility screening and report generation
Employs LLMs to draft informed consent language and regulatory responses
Relies on AI alerts for patient safety monitoring and protocol deviation detection

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-based patient prescreening flagged previously overlooked eligible candidates, improving recruitment rates
An LLM-generated regulatory response needed significant manual revision due to lack of context sensitivity
Learned to always verify AI-generated data for accuracy before submission

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Has access to periodic workshops and online training modules for new AI tools
Peers often share tips and best practices through internal forums or chat groups
Onboarding for AI software is sometimes supplemented with vendor-led webinars

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex tasks to maintain oversight
Uses AI independently for routine data analysis and report generation
Values the option to override or refine AI-generated outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant that augments but does not replace core responsibilities
Occasionally treats AI as a co-worker for routine regulatory documentation
Relies on AI-generated suggestions but retains final decision authority

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to adopting new AI-driven workflows when they streamline documentation or patient tracking
Expects clear demonstration of efficiency gains before fully committing to new workflows
Prefers pilot programs before full-scale implementation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Favors conversational interfaces for drafting documents or resolving queries
Uses command-based interfaces for structured data entry and report requests
Appreciates user interfaces that allow easy switching between modes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks, such as assessing eligibility nuances, require clinical judgment not easily captured by AI
Regulatory submissions often demand experience-based interpretation of guidelines
Patient interactions benefit from empathetic communication developed over years of practice

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated outputs for initial screening and routine documentation
Maintains healthy skepticism for critical safety or regulatory decisions, always reviewing AI recommendations
Trust increases when AI tools provide transparent reasoning or audit trails

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive, data-driven tasks such as prescreening and basic reporting
Believes human oversight is essential for consent, safety adjudication, and protocol deviation management
Supports hybrid workflows where AI prepares drafts and humans review/approve

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable when robust validation and manual review checkpoints are in place
Feels automation is threatening if it bypasses regulatory or ethical safeguards
Prefers automation to be phased in with clear user control options

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and oversight skills
Routine data entry skills are less critical due to automation
Communication and regulatory expertise remain essential

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing proficiency in interpreting AI-generated insights and reports
Learning to validate and audit AI-driven processes for compliance
Participating in user feedback for tool development and optimization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about AI replacing repetitive administrative tasks
Confident that core coordinator responsibilities requiring human judgment are secure
Monitors industry trends to stay ahead of potential role changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated reports are subject to manual review before submission
Can override AI-recommended actions in patient safety or regulatory matters
Organization mandates regular audits of AI decision logs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team policies increasingly encourage trial adoption of AI tools for routine tasks
Company offers guidelines for responsible AI use and data privacy
Feedback loops have been established to refine AI workflows based on user experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a transformative force in clinical research, improving trial efficiency and data quality
Believes AI will enable more personalized and accessible healthcare
Acknowledges risks of bias and over-reliance on automation in sensitive areas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Prioritizes patient privacy and informed consent in all AI applications
Worries about potential for algorithmic bias in patient screening tools
Believes transparent validation and human oversight are ethical necessities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI that integrates seamlessly with all EHR and CTMS platforms
Desires tools with better transparency and explainability of AI decisions
Seeks improved predictive analytics for patient adherence and dropout risk

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates a shift toward more oversight and quality management roles
Expects routine administrative tasks to be almost entirely automated
Foresees increased collaboration with data scientists and AI specialists

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured pilot programs for new AI tools with user feedback cycles
Ensure all AI systems are validated for accuracy, fairness, and regulatory compliance
Provide ongoing training and support for staff adapting to AI-driven workflows
Develop clear policies for data privacy and ethical AI use
